enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source platform for file storage, sharing, synchronization, and real-time document collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sync-in is an open-source, self-hosted platform for file storage, sharing, synchronization, and collaborative document editing, positioned as a sovereign alternative to commercial cloud storage and to other self-hosted suites like Nextcloud.

It integrates with Collabora Online, OnlyOffice, and Euro-Office for real-time in-browser document editing, and offers desktop sync clients for Windows, macOS, and Linux alongside WebDAV support for mounting storage as a network drive. The server component is licensed under AGPL-3.0 and distributed as Docker images.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Sync-in targets organizations that need to keep control of their data, including public-sector bodies, legal and engineering firms, NGOs, and IT teams running distributed or multi-site environments who want an auditable, self-managed alternative to Dropbox, Google Drive, or Nextcloud.

features.title: Collaborative document editing
features.description: Real-time co-editing of Office and PDF documents via Collabora Online, OnlyOffice, or Euro-Office integrations.

features.title: Desktop and CLI sync clients
features.description: Native synchronization clients for Windows, macOS, and Linux keep local folders in sync with the server.

features.title: WebDAV support
features.description: Mount Sync-in storage as a network drive in file explorers, mobile apps, and third-party WebDAV clients.

features.title: Spaces and guest access
features.description: Structured team spaces with granular access controls, plus restricted guest accounts for external collaborators.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Search across Office documents, PDFs, and other file formats stored on the platform.

features.title: Modern authentication
features.description: Supports local accounts, OIDC, LDAP, multi-factor authentication, recovery codes, and application passwords.

pros: Fully open source under AGPL-3.0 with no vendor lock-in
Self-hosted via Docker, giving organizations full data control
Built-in real-time office document collaboration
Flexible authentication (LDAP, OIDC, MFA) suited to enterprise and public-sector environments
cons: Younger project (public release in 2025) with a smaller community than established alternatives like Nextcloud
Requires self-hosting expertise; no official managed SaaS offering advertised on the site
Document co-editing depends on separately deployed Collabora/OnlyOffice servers

faqs.question: Is Sync-in free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sync-in's server software is free and open source (AGPL-3.0). Enterprise support options are offered separately by the project.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Sync-in?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sync-in is designed to be self-hosted and is distributed as Docker images for on-premise or private-cloud deployment.

faqs.question: Does Sync-in support real-time document editing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Collabora Online, OnlyOffice, or Euro-Office for collaborative editing of Office and PDF documents.

faqs.question: What platforms are supported?
faqs.answer: Sync-in offers a web interface plus desktop sync clients for Windows, macOS, and Linux, and it supports WebDAV for mounting as a network drive.

use_cases.title: Sovereign file storage for the public sector
use_cases.description: Government and municipal organizations self-host Sync-in to keep sensitive documents on infrastructure they control.

use_cases.title: Client document collaboration for professional firms
use_cases.description: Legal and engineering firms use guest access and spaces to share and co-edit documents with external clients under controlled permissions.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sync-in is an open-source, self-hosted platform for file storage, sync, sharing, and real-time document collaboration.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, Amazon S3-compatible object storage server you can self-host on-premises or in Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Zenko CloudServer is an open-source object storage server, developed by Scality, that implements the Amazon S3 API. It lets developers run S3-compatible storage on their own infrastructure and use existing S3 tooling and SDKs without changing application code.

CloudServer stores objects in their raw form without additional compression or splitting, so data placed in it remains readable through standard cloud storage browsers and S3 client tools. It is distributed under the Apache License 2.0 and can be deployed in minutes as a Docker container.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CloudServer suits developers building or testing S3-based applications locally, and organizations that want on-premises object storage compatible with the S3 API to avoid cloud vendor lock-in or to bridge multiple storage backends behind a single API.

features.title: Amazon S3 API compatibility
features.description: Implements the S3 API so existing S3 SDKs, CLI tools, and applications work against a self-hosted backend without code changes.

features.title: Multi-cloud backend support
features.description: Can be configured to use AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, or local storage as underlying backends.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker image, allowing a working S3-compatible server to be running within minutes.

features.title: Raw object storage
features.description: Stores uploaded data without post-processing such as compression or splitting, keeping it directly readable.

features.title: Language SDK support
features.description: Works with existing S3 SDKs for Python, Java, Node.js, and .NET.

pros: Free and open source under Apache 2.0
Drop-in compatibility with existing S3 tooling and SDKs
Fast to deploy via Docker
Can bridge multiple cloud storage backends behind one API
cons: Requires self-hosting and operational management (backups, scaling, security)
Advanced multi-site replication and management features are tied to Scality's broader Zenko/RING ecosystem
Documentation and community activity are smaller than major cloud storage providers

faqs.question: Is Zenko CloudServer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CloudServer is open-source software released under the Apache License 2.0 and is free to download and self-host.

faqs.question: Is it compatible with the Amazon S3 API?
faqs.answer: Yes, CloudServer implements the S3 API, so applications and SDKs written for S3 can point at a self-hosted CloudServer instance instead.

faqs.question: How is it deployed?
faqs.answer: CloudServer is distributed as a Docker image and can also be run directly with Node.js.

faqs.question: Who maintains it?
faqs.answer: CloudServer is developed by Scality as part of its Zenko multi-cloud data controller project and is available on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Local S3 development environment
use_cases.description: Developers run CloudServer locally in Docker to test S3-dependent application code without touching a live AWS account.

use_cases.title: On-premises S3-compatible storage
use_cases.description: Enterprises deploy CloudServer on their own infrastructure to get S3 API compatibility while keeping data in-house.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Zenko CloudServer is a free, open-source object storage server offering Amazon S3 API compatibility for self-hosted deployments.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, vendor-neutral OCI-compliant container image and artifact registry built in Go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Zot is a production-ready, vendor-neutral OCI (Open Container Initiative) image registry. It stores container images and artifacts in the OCI image format and communicates using the OCI distribution specification, so it works as a drop-in replacement wherever a standard OCI/Docker registry is expected.

The project is written in Go and released under the Apache-2.0 license. It is commonly deployed via Kubernetes and Helm as a self-hosted, scale-out registry for storing container images and other OCI artifacts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Zot is aimed at platform and DevOps teams who need a lightweight, standards-compliant container registry they can self-host, integrate into Kubernetes clusters, and run at scale without depending on a proprietary registry vendor.

features.title: OCI distribution spec compliance
features.description: Implements the OCI distribution specification for storing and serving container images and artifacts.

features.title: Vendor-neutral registry
features.description: Not tied to any single cloud provider, giving teams a portable self-hosted registry.

features.title: Kubernetes and Helm support
features.description: Designed to deploy into Kubernetes environments and integrate with Helm-based workflows.

features.title: Scale-out deployment
features.description: Built for production use cases that require scaling the registry horizontally.

features.title: Security scorecard tracking
features.description: Maintains public security posture indicators such as OpenSSF Scorecard and CII Best Practices badges.

pros: Free and open source under Apache-2.0
Standards-based OCI distribution spec compliance for broad tool compatibility
Lightweight, single Go binary makes deployment simple
Actively maintained with public security posture tracking
cons: Requires self-hosting and operational management like any self-run registry
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to registries like Docker Hub or Harbor
Advanced feature availability depends on keeping up with frequent releases

faqs.question: Is Zot free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Zot is open-source software released under the Apache-2.0 license.

faqs.question: Is Zot compatible with Docker and OCI tooling?
faqs.answer: Yes, it implements the OCI distribution specification, making it compatible with standard OCI/Docker registry clients.

faqs.question: How is Zot typically deployed?
faqs.answer: Zot is commonly deployed via Kubernetes and Helm, or run as a standalone Go binary, for self-hosted use.

faqs.question: What language is Zot written in?
faqs.answer: Zot is written primarily in Go.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Kubernetes image registry
use_cases.description: Platform teams run Zot inside their clusters via Helm as an internal, vendor-neutral container registry.

use_cases.title: Vendor-neutral artifact storage
use_cases.description: Teams store OCI artifacts (not just container images) in Zot to avoid dependence on a single cloud registry provider.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Zot is a free, open-source, vendor-neutral OCI-compliant container registry built in Go for self-hosted deployments.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Node.js BitTorrent tracker implementation with HTTP, UDP, and WebSocket server and client support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: bittorrent-tracker is an open-source Node.js implementation of a BitTorrent tracker, providing both client and server code. A BitTorrent tracker is a service that BitTorrent clients report to and query for peer lists, helping peers find each other within a swarm.

The package supports HTTP trackers, UDP trackers (BEP 15), and WebTorrent-compatible WebSocket trackers, over both IPv4 and IPv6. It can be installed globally via npm and run as a standalone self-hosted tracker server from the command line, or embedded programmatically in a Node.js application. It is maintained by Feross Aboukhadijeh and WebTorrent, LLC under the MIT license, and is used by clients such as WebTorrent and peerflix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: This project is for developers who need to run their own BitTorrent or WebTorrent tracker infrastructure, such as for private torrent swarms, WebTorrent-based applications, or peer discovery in custom P2P tools.

features.title: HTTP, UDP, and WebSocket tracker support
features.description: Runs multiple tracker protocols simultaneously, including BEP 15 UDP trackers and WebTorrent-style WebSocket trackers.

features.title: Command-line tracker server
features.description: Can be launched instantly from the CLI with `bittorrent-tracker`, starting HTTP, UDP, and WS servers together.

features.title: Programmatic client and server API
features.description: Exposes a JavaScript API for embedding tracker client or server logic directly into Node.js applications.

features.title: IPv4 and IPv6 support
features.description: Handles peer announcements and scraping over both IP versions.

features.title: Built-in stats endpoint
features.description: Serves tracker statistics via a web page at /stats or as JSON at /stats.json.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Simple to self-host as a standalone tracker via a single CLI command
Supports the full range of modern tracker protocols (HTTP/UDP/WebSocket)
Actively maintained and used by well-known projects like WebTorrent
cons: It is a developer library/CLI tool, not a hosted service or GUI application
Requires Node.js and command-line familiarity to deploy
No built-in web dashboard beyond basic stats reporting

faqs.question: Is bittorrent-tracker free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open-source software released under the MIT license and free to use.

faqs.question: Can I self-host my own BitTorrent tracker with this?
faqs.answer: Yes, installing it via npm and running the `bittorrent-tracker` command starts a self-hosted tracker server supporting HTTP, UDP, and WebSocket protocols.

faqs.question: Who maintains this project?
faqs.answer: It is maintained by Feross Aboukhadijeh and WebTorrent, LLC, and is used as the tracker implementation behind WebTorrent.

faqs.question: Does it support WebTorrent (browser-based) peers?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a WebSocket tracker server compatible with WebTorrent's browser-based peer connections.

use_cases.title: Private torrent swarm tracking
use_cases.description: Developers run their own tracker instance to coordinate peers for a private or custom torrent distribution.

use_cases.title: WebTorrent application backend
use_cases.description: Web apps built on WebTorrent use this tracker to enable browser-to-browser peer discovery over WebSockets.
use_cases.audience: Web developers building P2P browser apps

enrichment.seo_meta_description: bittorrent-tracker is a free, open-source Node.js BitTorrent tracker server and client supporting HTTP, UDP, and WebSocket.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with a client-server architecture and web, GTK, and console interfaces.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Deluge is a free and open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client written in Python, using libtorrent for its core networking logic. Since version 1.0 it has used a client-server (daemon) architecture, letting the download engine run headless on one machine while being controlled remotely through a GTK desktop interface, a web UI, or a console interface.

Most of Deluge's advanced functionality is delivered through a plugin system, which developers have used to extend the client with features beyond the base install. It is released under the GPL-3.0-or-later license and is commonly self-hosted on NAS devices, servers, and seedboxes for always-on downloading.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Deluge suits users who want a lightweight, headless BitTorrent client they can run on a home server, NAS, or seedbox and control remotely through a web browser, rather than a client tied to a single desktop session.

features.title: Client-server architecture
features.description: Runs the download daemon separately from the interface, enabling remote control of a headless server instance.

features.title: Multiple interfaces
features.description: Offers GTK desktop, web-based, and console (text) interfaces to the same underlying daemon.

features.title: Plugin system
features.description: Extends functionality through community-developed plugins covering scheduling, notifications, label management, and more.

features.title: libtorrent-based engine
features.description: Uses the libtorrent C++ library for efficient, protocol-compliant BitTorrent networking.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

pros: Completely free and open source
Runs headlessly with a web UI, well-suited to servers and NAS devices
Plugin ecosystem for extending core functionality
Lightweight resource usage
cons: Interface feels dated compared to some newer BitTorrent clients
Plugin quality and maintenance vary by plugin author
Setting up the daemon/remote UI split requires more configuration than a simple desktop client

faqs.question: Is Deluge free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Deluge is free, open-source software licensed under GPL-3.0-or-later.

faqs.question: Can Deluge run headless on a server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Deluge's client-server architecture lets the daemon run headlessly on a server or NAS while being controlled via the web UI or a remote GTK client.

faqs.question: What platforms does Deluge support?
faqs.answer: Deluge runs on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

faqs.question: Does Deluge support plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, most advanced features are delivered via a plugin system that extends the core client.

use_cases.title: Always-on home server torrenting
use_cases.description: Users run the Deluge daemon on a NAS or home server and manage downloads remotely through the web interface.

use_cases.title: Seedbox torrent management
use_cases.description: Users deploy Deluge on a remote seedbox for continuous seeding and downloading independent of a local machine.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Deluge is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with headless daemon mode, web UI, and a plugin system.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, browser-based peer-to-peer file and clipboard sharing tool built on WebRTC, with no size limits or sign-up.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PrivyDrop is an open-source, privacy-focused peer-to-peer file and text-clipboard sharing tool that transfers data directly between browsers using WebRTC, without routing file contents through a central server. It works as an AirDrop-style tool that works across any device with a browser, not just Apple hardware.

It requires no registration and has no advertised file size or speed limits. Transfers can resume after interruption, and rooms can be shared via link or QR code with support for multiple simultaneous receivers and folder transfers. The project is MIT licensed, with a frontend built on Next.js, React, and TypeScript and a Node.js/Express backend using Redis and Socket.IO for signaling; it can be self-hosted via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: PrivyDrop is aimed at users who want a quick, private way to move files or text between devices or with other people without creating an account, uploading to a third-party cloud, or dealing with file-size caps, and at self-hosters who want to run their own instance for full control.

features.title: Peer-to-peer WebRTC transfer
features.description: Sends files and text directly between browsers using WebRTC rather than storing data on a central server.

features.title: No registration required
features.description: Users can create and join a transfer room instantly without an account.

features.title: Resumable transfers
features.description: Interrupted transfers can resume from where they left off instead of restarting.

features.title: Multi-receiver rooms
features.description: A single share can be picked up by multiple receivers connected to the same room.

features.title: Rich text clipboard sharing
features.description: Shares formatted text/clipboard content alongside files, not just raw files.

features.title: Self-hostable via Docker
features.description: Can be deployed on your own server with a single-command Docker setup, including automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
No account, file-size limits, or speed limits advertised
Direct peer-to-peer transfer avoids storing files on a third-party server
Self-hostable for full control over the deployment
cons: Peer-to-peer transfer requires both sender and receiver to be online simultaneously
Relies on browser WebRTC support and network conditions (NAT/firewall) that can affect connectivity
Smaller community/track record compared to established file-transfer tools

faqs.question: Is PrivyDrop free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PrivyDrop is free to use and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Do I need to create an account?
faqs.answer: No, PrivyDrop does not require registration to send or receive files and text.

faqs.question: How does PrivyDrop transfer files?
faqs.answer: It uses WebRTC to send data directly between browsers peer-to-peer, rather than uploading files to a central server.

faqs.question: Can I self-host PrivyDrop?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be self-hosted via Docker, including configurations for public domains with automatic HTTPS.

use_cases.title: Quick cross-device file transfer
use_cases.description: Users move photos, documents, or clipboard text between their own devices without cables or cloud uploads.

use_cases.title: Private ad-hoc sharing with others
use_cases.description: Users share a room link or QR code to send files directly to another person without either party creating an account.
use_cases.audience: People sharing files privately with others

enrichment.seo_meta_description: PrivyDrop is a free, open-source, browser-based peer-to-peer file and clipboard sharing tool built on WebRTC.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with a built-in web UI, RSS reader, and torrent search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: qBittorrent is a free, open-source BitTorrent client written in native C++ using the Qt toolkit and the libtorrent-rasterbar library. It was originally created by Christophe Dumez in 2006 as an ad-free alternative to µTorrent, and today runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and FreeBSD with a consistent feature set across platforms.

It includes bandwidth scheduling, torrent queuing and prioritization, per-file content selection within torrents, an integrated RSS feed reader with automatic download filters, and a torrent search engine powered by installable plugins that queries multiple torrent sites at once. Remote management is available through a secure web UI, and it supports IPv6, Unicode, and roughly 70 languages. It is released under GPL-2.0 (with an OpenSSL linking exception).

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: qBittorrent suits users who want a full-featured, ad-free BitTorrent client with strong remote-management options via its web UI, making it popular for both desktop use and headless/server deployments such as NAS boxes and seedboxes.

features.title: Web UI for remote control
features.description: Manage torrents remotely through a secure browser-based interface, suited to headless server installs.

features.title: Integrated RSS reader
features.description: Subscribes to RSS feeds and applies automatic download filters to fetch new torrents.

features.title: Built-in torrent search
features.description: Searches across multiple torrent sites simultaneously using installable Python-based search plugins.

features.title: Bandwidth scheduling
features.description: Sets time-based upload/download speed limits to manage network usage.

features.title: Sequential downloading
features.description: Downloads torrent pieces in order to enable approximate media streaming while the torrent is still downloading.

features.title: Torrent queuing and prioritization
features.description: Controls the order and priority in which multiple torrents and their files download.

pros: Completely free and open source with no ads
Full-featured web UI ideal for remote/server use
Built-in RSS automation and torrent search reduce the need for external tools
Available on all major desktop and server platforms
cons: Interface can feel less polished than some paid alternatives
Built-in search plugins depend on third-party torrent site availability
Resource usage can be heavier than minimalist clients like Transmission

faqs.question: Is qBittorrent free?
faqs.answer: Yes, qBittorrent is free, open-source software licensed under GPL-2.0 with an OpenSSL exception.

faqs.question: Does qBittorrent have a web interface?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a secure web UI for remotely managing torrents, commonly used for headless server or NAS installs.

faqs.question: What platforms does qBittorrent support?
faqs.answer: qBittorrent runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and FreeBSD.

faqs.question: Does qBittorrent include a search feature?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has a built-in torrent search engine that uses installable plugins to query multiple torrent sites at once.

use_cases.title: Server/NAS torrent management
use_cases.description: Users run qBittorrent headlessly on a NAS or server and manage downloads through the web UI from anywhere on their network.

use_cases.title: Automated RSS-based downloading
use_cases.description: Users set up RSS feed subscriptions with download filters so new torrents matching their criteria download automatically.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: qBittorrent is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with a web UI, RSS reader, and built-in torrent search.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, lightweight BitTorrent client for macOS, Windows, and Linux with minimal resource usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Transmission is a free, open-source BitTorrent client built for low resource consumption while remaining full-featured. It offers native interfaces on each platform: a GTK interface following GNOME design guidelines on Linux, a Qt-based interface on Windows, and a native macOS app with Apple Silicon support.

Core features include watch directories, bad-peer blocklists, tracker editing, speed limits, and a built-in web interface for remote control. Because of its light footprint, Transmission has been embedded by NAS and router manufacturers including Western Digital, Zyxel, and Belkin, and was historically Ubuntu's default BitTorrent client for its accessible learning curve.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Transmission is a good fit for users who want a simple, low-overhead BitTorrent client for personal use, and for NAS or embedded-device use cases where a minimal resource footprint matters.

features.title: Native platform apps
features.description: Ships as a native GTK app on Linux, a Qt app on Windows, and a native macOS app with Apple Silicon support.

features.title: Web interface
features.description: Includes a built-in web UI for remote monitoring and control of the daemon.

features.title: Watch directories
features.description: Automatically starts torrents added to a designated watch folder.

features.title: Bad peer blocklists
features.description: Supports blocklists to filter out known bad peers.

features.title: Speed limits and tracker editing
features.description: Lets users cap transfer speeds and edit tracker lists per torrent.

pros: Free, open source, and ad-free
Extremely lightweight, well suited to NAS and embedded devices
Simple, approachable interface with a low learning curve
Widely embedded by hardware vendors, indicating strong stability
cons: Fewer advanced features (e.g., built-in torrent search) compared to clients like qBittorrent
Less frequent major UI overhauls than some competitors
No official mobile app maintained by the core project

faqs.question: Is Transmission free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Transmission is free, open-source software with no paid tiers or ads.

faqs.question: What platforms does Transmission support?
faqs.answer: Transmission has native apps for macOS (including Apple Silicon), Windows, and Linux.

faqs.question: Can I control Transmission remotely?
faqs.answer: Yes, Transmission includes a built-in web interface for remote monitoring and control.

faqs.question: Why do NAS devices often include Transmission?
faqs.answer: Its low resource usage and stability have made it a common choice for manufacturers like Western Digital, Zyxel, and Belkin to bundle on their devices.

use_cases.title: Lightweight desktop torrenting
use_cases.description: Users who want a simple, low-resource torrent client for everyday personal downloading choose Transmission.

use_cases.title: Embedded NAS/router torrent client
use_cases.description: Hardware vendors and self-hosters run Transmission on NAS devices or routers due to its minimal footprint.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Transmission is a free, open-source, lightweight BitTorrent client for macOS, Windows, and Linux with a web interface.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted platform for one-time temporary file and text upload and sharing, no registration required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: 015 is an open-source, self-hosted platform focused on temporary, one-time file and text sharing. It lets users upload files or share text without creating an account, and features chunked uploads with client-side hashing so identical files can be recognized and transferred instantly.

The project's frontend is built with Vue 3, Nuxt 3, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS, with a Go (Echo framework) backend using Redis and Asynq for background processing. It is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, ships with Docker Compose for self-hosted deployment, and its source is maintained on the developer's own Gitea and GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: 015 fits individuals and small teams who want a lightweight, self-hosted, no-registration way to share files or text temporarily, such as a personal or small-community alternative to public temp-file services.

features.title: Chunked uploads with instant transfer
features.description: Splits large files into chunks and hashes them client-side to detect duplicates and speed up repeat uploads.

features.title: No registration required
features.description: Users can upload and share files or text without creating an account.

features.title: Password protection and share limits
features.description: Shares can be protected with a password and limited in number of downloads.

features.title: Resume upload support
features.description: Interrupted uploads can resume rather than restarting from scratch.

features.title: Image compression
features.description: Includes built-in image compression and format handling for uploaded images.

features.title: File preview
features.description: Previews multiple file types directly in the browser before download.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL-3.0
No account required to share files or text
Docker Compose setup makes self-hosting straightforward
Modern, actively developed tech stack (Vue 3/Nuxt 3/Go)
cons: Small community project with limited English-language documentation
Designed for temporary/one-time sharing rather than long-term storage
The public demo is hosted on a personal domain, so evaluating it may require self-hosting your own instance

faqs.question: Is 015 free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, 015 is open-source software released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use 015?
faqs.answer: No, 015 is designed for one-time, no-registration file and text sharing.

faqs.question: Can I self-host 015?
faqs.answer: Yes, it ships with Docker Compose configuration for self-hosted deployment.

faqs.question: Is 015 meant for permanent file storage?
faqs.answer: No, it is built for temporary, one-time uploads and sharing rather than long-term storage.

use_cases.title: Quick, anonymous file sharing
use_cases.description: Users share a file or text snippet with someone without setting up accounts on either side.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted temp-file service for a community
use_cases.description: Small teams or communities self-host their own instance instead of relying on third-party temp-file websites.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: 015 is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for temporary, no-registration file and text sharing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted file upload and vault service with ShareX support, albums, and a masonry media browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chibisafe is an open-source, self-hosted file upload service described by its creators as a beautiful and performant vault for storing files in the cloud. It can run in public, user-account, or invite-only modes, and presents uploaded media in a masonry-style browsing interface.

The project is written in TypeScript, licensed under MIT, and is recommended to be deployed via Docker (Docker Compose) behind a reverse proxy such as Caddy or Nginx. It supports S3-compatible storage backends in addition to local disk storage, and offers a REST API for automated uploads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Chibisafe suits individuals and communities who want to self-host their own file/image hosting service, particularly as a ShareX-compatible upload destination for screenshots, or as a private alternative to public file-hosting sites.

features.title: ShareX integration
features.description: Works as a custom uploader destination for the ShareX screenshot tool on Windows.

features.title: Chunked uploads
features.description: Uploads large files in chunks to reduce the impact of network failures.

features.title: Albums and shareable links
features.description: Organizes uploads into albums/folders with shareable links.

features.title: User management and quotas
features.description: Supports public, account-based, or invite-only access with per-user storage quotas.

features.title: Built-in URL shortener
features.description: Includes a link-shortening feature alongside file hosting.

features.title: S3 storage support
features.description: Can store uploaded files on S3-compatible object storage instead of local disk.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Provides an API for programmatic uploads and integrations, including iOS Shortcuts support.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
ShareX and iOS Shortcuts integrations make it easy to plug into existing screenshot/upload workflows
Supports S3-compatible storage for scalable self-hosted deployments
Active community via Discord and GitHub
cons: Requires self-hosting and reverse-proxy setup for production use
Migrating between major versions requires manual data-folder migration steps
No official managed/hosted version offered by the project

faqs.question: Is Chibisafe free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chibisafe is free, open-source software released under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does Chibisafe work with ShareX?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports ShareX as a custom upload destination for screenshots and files.

faqs.question: Can Chibisafe use S3 storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports S3-compatible object storage in addition to local disk storage.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Chibisafe?
faqs.answer: Docker (via Docker Compose) is the recommended deployment method, typically behind a reverse proxy like Caddy or Nginx.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted screenshot hosting
use_cases.description: Users configure ShareX to upload screenshots directly to their own Chibisafe instance instead of a third-party host.

use_cases.title: Private file vault for a community
use_cases.description: Communities run Chibisafe in invite-only or account mode to give members a private, quota-managed file storage space.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chibisafe is a free, open-source, self-hosted file upload and vault service with ShareX support and an API.
